skill.name: azure-storage-blob-java
skill.description: Build Java applications that upload, download, list, copy, and manage Azure Blob Storage data with the Azure Storage Blob SDK.
skill.summary: Use Azure Storage Blob SDK patterns for Java blob, container, streaming, metadata, SAS, and proxy workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static external-command alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, not executable Ruby or shell backticks. The weak cryptography alert at SKILL.md:3 is also a false positive, while the Azure endpoint example is legitimate network configuration. The skill is publishable with low risk because it teaches networked storage operations, local file transfer, and credential placeholder usage.

low_findings.title: False positive external command detections
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at Markdown fence lines. These lines delimit XML, Java, and Bash examples and do not execute commands inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are fenced Markdown examples, and no Ruby or shell backtick execution syntax appears in the skill content. The context is instructional SDK documentation rather than executable automation.

low_findings.title: Credential and data transfer examples need careful use
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples for connection strings, SAS tokens, file uploads, downloads, and URL-based blob copy. These are legitimate Azure SDK patterns, but users must avoid hardcoded secrets and unintended file exposure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly handle storage credentials and local files, which can affect confidentiality if copied without review. The placeholders and standard Azure SDK APIs indicate legitimate intent rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: False positive weak cryptography detection
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alert points to the frontmatter description, which only describes Azure Blob Storage usage. No weak cryptographic algorithm appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md:3 contains plain descriptive text and no cryptographic API, algorithm name, or implementation. The static alert has no supporting semantic evidence.

low_findings.title: Benign hardcoded Azure endpoint example
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL alert is an environment variable example for an Azure Blob service endpoint. It uses a placeholder account name and does not send data to an unknown host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a documented Azure Blob endpoint pattern with a placeholder account value. No evidence found of data exfiltration, tracking, or hidden network destinations.

content.user_title: Build Azure Blob Storage Java Apps
content.value_statement: Java teams often need correct Azure Blob Storage patterns for files, streams, metadata, and access tokens. This skill provides concise SDK examples for common container and blob workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for the Azure Storage Blob SDK for Java.
Demonstrates BlobServiceClient, BlobContainerClient, and BlobClient creation patterns.
Provides examples for uploading, downloading, streaming, listing, deleting, and copying blobs.
Covers blob metadata, HTTP headers, leases, SAS token generation, and request conditions.
Includes error handling, proxy configuration, and environment variable examples.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not run Azure operations by itself.
It does not validate user permissions, storage account policies, or network access.
Examples use placeholders and must be adapted before production use.
It does not provide complete application architecture or automated tests.

use_cases.title: Add Blob Uploads to a Java Service
use_cases.description: Use client creation and upload examples to add file storage to an existing Java application.

use_cases.title: Review Azure Storage Access Patterns
use_cases.description: Compare connection string, SAS token, and DefaultAzureCredential examples before choosing an authentication approach.

use_cases.title: Prototype Storage Maintenance Tasks
use_cases.description: Use listing, metadata, copy, delete, and lease examples to draft operational storage workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Blob Client
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for Java storage access
prompt_templates.prompt: Using this skill, show me the simplest Java setup for creating a BlobServiceClient and BlobClient for my storage account.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Upload and Download
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline Java code for uploading a local file to Azure Blob Storage and downloading it later.

prompt_templates.title: Add Metadata and Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a Java blob workflow that sets metadata, handles missing blobs, and avoids overwriting existing data.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Secure SAS-Based Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to compare blob-level and container-level SAS token examples, then recommend a least-privilege approach for temporary downloads.

output_examples.input: I need to upload reports from a Java service to Azure Blob Storage.
output: A recommended client creation approach for the service.
An upload flow using file or stream APIs.
Notes about overwrite behavior, metadata, and credential handling.

output_examples.input: I need to list blobs under a virtual folder and read metadata.
output: A listing approach using prefix or hierarchy options.
Guidance for retrieving metadata and blob properties.
A warning to validate naming rules and access permissions.

output_examples.input: I need a temporary download link for one blob.
output: A blob-level SAS strategy with read-only permissions.
A short expiry recommendation for temporary access.
A reminder to avoid logging or committing generated tokens.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential or managed identity when the deployment environment supports it.
Use least-privilege SAS permissions with short expirations for temporary access.
Validate file paths, blob names, and overwrite settings before moving user data.
anti_patterns: Do not commit connection strings, SAS tokens, or generated access links to source control.
Do not grant container-level SAS access when a blob-level token is sufficient.
Do not copy examples into production without adding logging, retries, and permission checks.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides Java SDK examples and guidance. The user or application executes any resulting code.

faq.question: Which authentication methods are covered?
faq.answer: It shows SAS tokens, connection strings, and DefaultAzureCredential patterns for Java clients.

faq.question: Can it help with streaming large files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes stream-based upload and download examples for blob data.

faq.question: Does it include SAS token guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows blob-level and container-level SAS token generation examples.

faq.question: Is this enough for production code?
faq.answer: No. Production systems still need validation, retries, monitoring, access reviews, and secure secret storage.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level low?
faq.answer: The static command and cryptography alerts are false positives, but the skill discusses network storage and local file handling.
